FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 2 follow-up data (N=9429), ages 11-12 years, cross-sectional analysis

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying measured via self-report questionnaire. Screen time modalities including internet and social media exposure reported: abstract states 'Total screen time, particularly on the internet and social media, was associated with both cyberbullying victimization and perpetration'

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple logistic regression analyses conducted; Table 3 reports associations between screen time modalities and cyberbullying outcomes with test statistics

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports adjusted odds ratios and 95% CIs for screen time associations: social media OR 1.21 (95% CI 1.15-1.27) for victimization; internet OR 1.28 (95% CI 1.13-1.45)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using ABCD baseline data with logistic regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes smartphone/SM as independent variable predicting non-SM outcome (cyberbullying). Cross-sectional analysis. E6 applies: cyberbullying behavior measured separately from phone/SM variables with phone/SM also as IV.
notes: Paper also reports sociodemographic predictors of cyberbullying (sex, race, income, education); these do not trigger FT-EC7 because the main analysis focuses on screen time → cyberbullying association. Cyberbullying outcome is non-SM-related.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD baseline data (2016-2018), N=10,755, ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures generic screen time use across 6 modalities (television, videos, video games, social networking, texting, video chat) but does NOT measure a SPECIFIC smartphone OR social media variable per prompt criteria. Study focuses on sociodemographic correlates of broad screen time categories, not on smartphone-specific or social media-specific exposure as primary variable. Fails requirement: 'Paper measures a SPECIFIC smartphone OR social media variable'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis with multiple linear regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in The Journal of Pediatrics, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: Excluded at FT_IC2; FT_EC7 not applicable
notes: Paper excluded due to failure at FT_IC2. While screen time modalities are measured, they are measured as generic categories (screen time by type) across all children, not as specific smartphone or social media exposure variables that meet inclusion threshold. This is a sociodemographic epidemiology study, not a smartphone/SM-specific study.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study December 2020 COVID-19 survey data, N=4,106 adolescents ages 12-14

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures exposure to COVID-19 related information via social media (9.1% reporting >1 hour daily), internet (4.3%), and television (10.2%). Social media and internet are SPECIFIC modalities; abstract: 'greater frequency of parental counseling and exposure to COVID-19 television or social media were associated with always masking behaviors'

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses with risk ratios reported; Table 2 shows adjusted associations between information sources and masking behavior

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports adjusted risk ratios and 95% CIs: social media ARR 1.09 (95% CI 1.00-1.19), television ARR 1.09 (95% CI 1.01-1.17), internet ARR 0.94 (95% CI 0.83-1.07)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD COVID-19 survey data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Preventive Medicine Reports, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: Social media and internet information exposure are independent variables predicting masking behavior (non-SM outcome). Cross-sectional. Additional confounders include parental education, income, household demographics—mixed predictor types beyond demographic-only. Paper does not fit FT-EC7 criteria.
notes: Paper examines multiple information sources (social media, internet, television, parental counseling) as predictors of COVID-19 masking behavior. Social media qualifies as SPECIFIC modality per FT_IC2 criteria.
